critique.summary: The article reports on a recent incident affecting multiple GitHub services including Actions, Issues, Pull Requests, Webhooks, Codespaces, and login functionality. The disruption caused elevated error rates and degraded performance but was later resolved, with a root cause analysis pending.
mainPoints: GitHub experienced elevated error rates impacting several services on March 24, 2026.
Affected services included Actions, Issues, Pull Requests, Webhooks, Codespaces, and login functionality.
GitHub acknowledged the issue, provided updates during the incident, and confirmed resolution by 20:56 UTC.
A detailed root cause analysis is promised to be shared once available.
critique.truthfulness: The information is consistent with official status updates from GitHub's own status page, which is a reliable primary source for service disruptions. The timeline and affected services match the publicly available incident report, indicating the article accurately reflects the facts about the outage and resolution.
considerations: The article does not provide the root cause analysis since it was not available at the time of posting.
There is no information on the impact on users beyond error rates and degraded performance, such as data loss or security implications.
No alternative perspectives or external expert commentary are included.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around GitHub's recurring downtime and reliability issues, with many users expressing frustration and skepticism about Microsoft's management and the platform's future. Several commenters suggest moving to self-hosted alternatives or preparing for a world without GitHub, while others speculate on causes such as AI-related coding quality or Microsoft’s broader product challenges.

comments.author: guywithabike
comments.text:
The worst part of all this is that GitHub's CTO and VP of Engineering sent out the usual "here's what we'll do to fix things" letter to their larger customers and, without exaggeration, it boiled down to: 1) "Here's a bunch of stuff we already did!" which... clearly isn't working, and 2) "We're continuing our Azure migration." also clearly not working.

So needless to say, if you depend on GitHub for critical business operations, you need to start thinking about what a world without GitHub looks like for your business and start working your way toward that. I know my confidence in GitHub's engineering leadership is at rock bottom.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed critique based on official communication and highlights a realistic concern about leadership and ongoing technical issues.

comments.author: bartread
comments.text:
Fuck sake. Again?

Sorry, I realise this comment isn't up to HN's usual standards for thoughtfulness and it is perhaps a bit inflammatory but... look, I'd bet the majority of us on this site rely on GitHub and I can't be the only one becoming incredibly frustrated with its recent unreliability[0]?

(And, yes, I did enough basic data analysis to confirm that it IS indeed getting worse versus a year, two years, and three years ago, and is particularly bad since the start of this year.)

[0] EDIT: clearly not from looking at the rest of the comments in this discussion.
comments.analysis: The comment is backed by data analysis and reflects a common user sentiment, making it a credible and insightful contribution.

comments.author: packetlost
comments.text:
I've been sitting here waiting for a critical deploy to happen via GitHub Actions (I know, hour fault, we should have left ages ago). My patience for this bullshit is gone, I'm going to be pushing very hard to get us off of GitHub entirely except for public code mirrors going forward.

Edit: oh look, their site says all good, but I still have jobs stuck. What a pile of garbage.

I'm so sick of this.
comments.analysis: A firsthand account of service degradation that aligns with reported issues, illustrating real user impact and motivating migration considerations.

comments.author: zelphirkalt
comments.text: Man, a while ago I thought: "It happens often, alright, but every 2 weeks? Sounds like a slight exaggeration." But it really is every 2 weeks, isn't it? If I imagine in a previous job anything production being down every 2 weeks ... phew, would have had to have a few hard talks and course corrections.
comments.analysis: This comment contextualizes the frequency of outages in a professional setting, emphasizing the severity of the problem.

comments.author: hirako2000
comments.text: I'm glad I moved over to forgejo. Being selfhosted, the UI loads faster. Most importantly, the thing is always responsive.
comments.analysis: Offers a practical alternative solution based on personal experience, contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: tholford
comments.text:
Setting up a Gitea instance is approachable, especially with agent assistance.

https://about.gitea.com/
comments.analysis: Provides helpful advice on self-hosting options, adding value for users considering migration.

comments.author: pylua
comments.text:
Anyone else notice other Microsoft cloud services ( for instance inside azure ) with bad performance also?

I can’t be specific but we are constantly complaining.
comments.analysis: Suggests a broader pattern of issues in Microsoft's cloud ecosystem, though lacking detailed evidence.

comments.author: proc0
comments.text:
It's starting to really look like the AI effect. It might be coincidence but I've noticed a lot more downtime and bad software lately. The last Nvidia drivers gave me a blue screen (last week or so), and speaking about Windows, I froze updates last year because it was clear they were introducing a bunch of issues with every update (not to mention unwanted features).

I like AI but actually not for coding because code quality is correlated to how well you understand the underlying systems you're building on, and AI is not really reasoning on this level at all. It's clearly synthesizing training data and it's useful in limited ways.
comments.analysis: While thoughtful, the claim linking AI to increased downtime is speculative and lacks concrete evidence.

comments.author: dsm4ck
comments.analysis: A brief, derogatory remark that adds little substantive value to the discussion.

comments.author: messe
comments.text:
For fucks sake.

I've been considering it for a while, but I'm definitely now pitching a move away from GitHub at our organization.
comments.analysis: Expresses a strong reaction and organizational decision, reflecting real consequences of GitHub's instability.
